% =============================================================
% MIGRATION STATUS (2026-05-27)
% This combined draft is the source-of-truth reference while the
% three spinoff papers are being constructed:
%   * papers/ipw/      Paper A (IPW + AC/Gwet, MCAR case)
%   * papers/fiml/     Paper B (FIML, MAR case)
%   * papers/quadratic/Paper C (closed-form quadratic = Lin's CCC)
% Per-section MIGRATED comments below mark which spinoff carries

% this draft until the relevant spinoff compiles and reads cleanly

% =============================================================
\begin{document}
\title{Agreement coefficients with missing raters}
\author{Jonas Moss}
\date{\today}
\address{Department of Data Science and Analytics, BI Norwegian Business School,
Oslo, Norway}
\begin{abstract}
We develop a framework for estimating chance-corrected agreement
coefficients (Cohen, Fleiss, Brennan--Prediger) when some ratings
are missing. Two data forms are considered. For rater-identified data
we present an inverse-probability-weighted (IPW) estimator that is
consistent under missing completely at random (MCAR), and a full-information
maximum-likelihood (FIML) estimator that is consistent under missing
at random (MAR) and attains the semiparametric efficiency bound. For
counts data with exchangeable raters we present the pairwise plug-in
estimator and a counts-FIML estimator based on hypergeometric completion
of partial counts. Joint asymptotic covariance matrices are given
for inference across coefficients. Two estimators commonly seen in
applied work, the available-case estimator and Gwet's reweighting,
are recorded as comparison baselines. The methods are implemented
in the R package \texttt{misskappa}.
\end{abstract}

\maketitle

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (IPW-only rewrite of Introduction)
\section{Introduction}

Inter-rater agreement is routinely summarised by a chance-corrected
coefficient: Cohen's $\kappa$ for two raters \citep{Cohen1968-sb},
Fleiss' $\kappa$ for many exchangeable raters \citep{Fleiss1971-to},
the Brennan--Prediger coefficient against a uniform reference \citep{Brennan1981-kf},
or weighted variants for ordinal or interval categories. When ratings
are incomplete, common practice is to apply these coefficients to
the pairwise-available data, or to reach for the default behaviour
of a software package such as \texttt{irrCAC} \citep{Gwet2014-pj}.
Neither approach has a missing-data theory behind it, and both can
mislead when the missingness mechanism is non-trivial.

This paper develops estimators of weighted kappa coefficients in the
presence of missing ratings. The treatment is organised by data form
and missingness assumption. For rater-identified (raw) data we describe
an inverse-probability-weighted (IPW) estimator of the Hájek type,
consistent under missing completely at random (MCAR), and a full-information
maximum-likelihood (FIML) estimator based on the discrete full-data
model, consistent under missing at random (MAR) and efficient in the
semiparametric sense. For counts data with exchangeable raters we
develop the corresponding pair: the pairwise plug-in estimator (an
IPW analogue under exchangeability) and a counts-FIML estimator that
treats the unobserved entries of each partial count vector as a
hypergeometrically-sampled completion. Two further estimators that
appear in applied work, the available-case estimator and Gwet's
reweighting, are recorded in a short comparison section. The closed-form
quadratic-loss estimator is noted as the one setting where pairwise
plug-in estimation attains efficiency.

Throughout we work in the semiparametric framework for missing data
\citep{Robins1994-jp,Tsiatis2006-sx,Van_der_Vaart2000-qc}. IPW estimation
is the natural translation of the Hájek estimator to chance-corrected
agreement coefficients, treating each observed rater pair as an inverse-
probability-weighted contribution. FIML reduces, on the discrete full-data
model, to maximum likelihood with the unobserved entries integrated
out via the EM algorithm, and Rubin's ignorability theorem \citep{Rubin1976-rb}
gives consistency under MAR. Influence-function inference yields joint
asymptotic covariance matrices across coefficients, which we use for
Wald-type confidence sets and for hypothesis tests of equality between
weighted kappas.

The contribution is twofold. First, the paper organises four estimators
into a coherent picture across the two data forms most commonly encountered
in practice. Second, the paper documents what can and cannot be inferred
from counts data: the full-composition distribution is not in general
identified from partial counts, but the kappa functional is identified,
and FIML on counts gives a consistent estimate of kappa even though
its $\theta$-iterate is not unique. The methods are implemented in
an R package \texttt{misskappa} with a single user-facing entry point;
the empirical example in Section \ref{sec:rpackage} demonstrates its
use.

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (full Setup section). Papers/fiml/ and
% papers/quadratic/ will carry their own trimmed Setup sections.
\section{Setup}

\label{sec:setup}

\subsection{Chance-corrected agreement}

A chance-corrected agreement coefficient takes the form $\kappa_{X}=1-d/d_{X}$,
where $d$ is a measure of observed disagreement and $d_{X}$ a measure
of chance disagreement. Let $X^{\star}=(X_{1}^{\star},\dots,X_{R}^{\star})$
be the rating vector of a randomly drawn subject by $R$ raters, with
$X_{j}^{\star}$ the rating of rater $j$. Observed disagreement is
\begin{equation}
d=\binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}E\!\left[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})\right],\label{eq:observed-disagreement}
\end{equation}
where $l(x,y)\geq0$ is a symmetric loss function with $l(x,x)=0$.
The three most common losses are the nominal ($l(x,y)=\mathbf{1}[x\neq y]$),
the absolute value ($l(x,y)=|x-y|$), and the quadratic ($l(x,y)=(x-y)^{2}$).

We use three variants of chance disagreement, of the Fleiss \citep{Fleiss1971-to},
Cohen / Conger \citep{Cohen1968-sb,Conger1980-co}, and Brennan--Prediger
\citep{Brennan1981-kf} types:
\begin{eqnarray}
d_{F} & = & R^{-2}\sum_{j,k}E\!\left[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\prime\star})\right],\label{eq:fleiss-chance}\\
d_{C} & = & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}E\!\left[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\prime\star})\right],\label{eq:conger-chance}\\
d_{BP} & = & C^{-2}\sum_{a,b}l(a,b),\label{eq:bp-chance}
\end{eqnarray}
where $X^{\prime\star}$ is an independent copy of $X^{\star}$. The
Brennan--Prediger variant assumes $X_{j}^{\star}$ takes values in
a finite set of $C$ categories. The corresponding population coefficients
are $\kappa_{X}=1-d/d_{X}$ for $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$; these definitions
reduce to Cohen's $\kappa$ when $R=2$ and to Fleiss' $\kappa$ when
raters are exchangeable.

For complete data the kappa coefficients are estimated by the sample
analogues; their joint asymptotic distribution is multinomial when
the rating model is discrete, and can be derived more generally via
$U$-statistics \citep{Moss2024-cc}.

\subsection{Missing-data model}

\label{sec:missingness}

The full data $X^{\star}=(X_{1}^{\star},\dots,X_{R}^{\star})$ is
not in general observed. Let $M=(M_{1},\dots,M_{R})\in\{0,1\}^{R}$
be a missingness indicator, with $M_{j}=1$ if rater $j$'s rating
for the subject is observed. The observed data is
\[
X_{j}=\begin{cases}
X_{j}^{\star} & M_{j}=1,\\
\mathrm{NA} & M_{j}=0,
\end{cases}
\]
where $\mathrm{NA}$ is a placeholder symbol that $X_{j}^{\star}$
does not take. Write $\pi_{j}=P(M_{j}=1)$ and $\pi_{jk}=P(M_{j}M_{k}=1)$
for the marginal and pairwise observation probabilities. Two standard
mechanisms appear in the sequel:
\begin{itemize}
\item \emph{Missing completely at random} (MCAR): $M$ is independent of
$X^{\star}$.
\item \emph{Missing at random} (MAR): $P(M=m\mid X^{\star})$ depends on
$X^{\star}$ only through the observed entries.
\end{itemize}
We assume throughout that $\pi_{jk}>0$ for all $j<k$, so every rater
pair contributes some observed pairs.

\subsection{Symbol glossary}

\label{sec:glossary}
\begin{description}
\item [{$n$}] Number of subjects.
\item [{$R$}] Number of raters.
\item [{$C$}] Number of categories (when finite).
\item [{$X_{ij}^{\star}$}] Rating of subject $i$ by rater $j$ (full data).
\item [{$M_{ij}$}] Indicator that $X_{ij}^{\star}$ is observed.
\item [{$X_{ij}$}] Observed rating ($X_{ij}^{\star}$ if $M_{ij}=1$, $\mathrm{NA}$
otherwise).
\item [{$l(x,y)$}] Pairwise loss function; $L=(l(a,b))_{a,b}$ its $C\times C$
matrix on the category set.
\item [{$d,d_{X}$}] Observed disagreement and chance disagreement, $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$.
\item [{$\kappa_{X}$}] Population coefficient, $1-d/d_{X}$.
\item [{$\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{S}$}] Estimator $S\in\{\mathrm{IPW},\mathrm{FIML},\mathrm{AC},\mathrm{G}\}$
of $\kappa_{X}$.
\item [{$\pi_{j},\pi_{jk}$}] Marginal and pairwise observation probabilities;
$\hat{\pi}_{j},\hat{\pi}_{jk}$ their sample counterparts.
\item [{$\theta$}] Probability mass function on $X^{\star}$ (raw form) or
on the composition simplex (counts form).
\item [{$Z_{i}$}] Full count vector $(Z_{i,1},\dots,Z_{i,C})$, $\sum_{c}Z_{i,c}=R$.
\item [{$N_{i}$}] Observed count vector with $\sum_{c}N_{i,c}=r_{i}\leq R$.
\end{description}

\section{Raw data}

\label{sec:raw}

We consider $n$ subjects rated by $R$ identified raters. The observed
data for subject $i$ is $(X_{i,1},\dots,X_{i,R})$ with possibly some
entries equal to $\mathrm{NA}$. Subjects are assumed independent
and identically distributed under the full-data model.

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (Sec 3.1 -> Paper A "Estimator and consistency")
\subsection{IPW (Hájek-type) estimator}

\label{sec:raw-ipw}

The marginal observation probabilities are estimated by $\hat{\pi}_{j}=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}M_{ij}$
and the pairwise observation probabilities by $\hat{\pi}_{jk}=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}M_{ij}M_{ik}$.
The Hájek-type IPW estimator is $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{IPW}}=1-\hat{d}^{\mathrm{IPW}}/\hat{d}_{X}^{\mathrm{IPW}}$,
with observed and chance components
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{d}^{\mathrm{IPW}} & = & \frac{\sum_{i}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij},X_{ik})M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}}{\sum_{i}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}},\\
\hat{d}_{C}^{\mathrm{IPW}} & = & \frac{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij},X_{i'k})M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}}{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}},
\end{eqnarray*}
and $\hat{d}_{F}^{\mathrm{IPW}}$ defined analogously to $\hat{d}_{C}^{\mathrm{IPW}}$
with summation over all $j,k$. The Brennan--Prediger version uses
$\hat{d}_{BP}$ from \eqref{eq:bp-chance}, which does not depend on
the data.

The estimator is a smooth function of $U$- and $V$-statistic averages
weighted by inverse observation probabilities. By the strong law of
large numbers, Slutsky's theorem, and the continuous mapping theorem
\citep{Van_der_Vaart2000-qc},
\begin{eqnarray}
\hat{d}^{\mathrm{IPW}} & \stackrel{a.s.}{\to} & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}\pi_{jk}^{-1}E\!\left[l(X_{j},X_{k})M_{j}M_{k}\right],\label{eq:ipw-limit}\\
\hat{d}_{X}^{\mathrm{IPW}} & \stackrel{a.s.}{\to} & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}\pi_{j}^{-1}\pi_{k}^{-1}E\!\left[l(X_{j},X_{k}')M_{j}M_{k}'\right],\nonumber
\end{eqnarray}
where $(X_{j}',M_{j}')$ is an independent copy of $(X_{j},M_{j})$
and similarly for $\hat{d}_{F}^{\mathrm{IPW}}$.
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:ipw-mcar}Under MCAR, $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{IPW}}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\kappa_{X}$
for $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$, provided $d_{X}>0$ and $\pi_{jk}>0$ for all
$j<k$.
\end{prop}
\begin{proof}
Under MCAR, $E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})M_{j}M_{k}]=\pi_{jk}E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})]$,
and equating $l(X_{j},X_{k})M_{j}M_{k}=l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})M_{j}M_{k}$
(extending the loss by $l(\cdot,\mathrm{NA})=0$) shows that the right-hand
side of \eqref{eq:ipw-limit} equals $\binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})]=d$.
The chance term is treated analogously using independence of $M_{j}$
and $M_{k}'$ across subjects, giving the corresponding $d_{X}$.
\end{proof}
The IPW estimator is the natural translation of the Hájek estimator
to chance-corrected agreement, with one critical detail: the observed-disagreement
component uses \emph{pairwise} weights $\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}$, while
the chance component uses \emph{marginal product} weights $\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}$.
This reflects the within-subject versus cross-subject structure of
the two terms.

% NOT migrated to Paper A. Sec 3.2 (raw FIML) is Paper B material.
\subsection{FIML estimator}

\label{sec:raw-fiml}

The full data $X^{\star}$ is treated as a draw from a probability
mass function $\theta$ on $\mathcal{X}=\{1,\dots,C\}^{R}$. Let $\theta_{r}=P(X^{\star}=r)$
for $r\in\mathcal{X}$. For an observed pattern $X_{i}$ with missing
positions $\mathcal{M}_{i}=\{j:M_{ij}=0\}$, the observed-data likelihood
factor is
\[
P(X_{i}\mid\theta,M_{i})=\sum_{r\in\mathcal{C}(X_{i})}\theta_{r},
\]
where $\mathcal{C}(X_{i})\subseteq\mathcal{X}$ is the set of full
patterns compatible with the observed entries. By Rubin's ignorability
theorem, when missingness is MAR the missing-data mechanism factors
out of the likelihood, and maximum likelihood proceeds by maximising
$\sum_{i}\log P(X_{i}\mid\theta,M_{i})$ in $\theta$ subject to $\sum_{r}\theta_{r}=1$.

The maximum-likelihood estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is computed by the
EM algorithm. At iteration $t$, the E-step computes posterior responsibilities
\[
q_{ir}^{(t)}=\frac{\theta_{r}^{(t)}\mathbf{1}[r\in\mathcal{C}(X_{i})]}{\sum_{s\in\mathcal{C}(X_{i})}\theta_{s}^{(t)}},
\]
and the M-step updates $\theta_{r}^{(t+1)}=n^{-1}\sum_{i}q_{ir}^{(t)}$.
The FIML kappa estimator is the plug-in $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}=1-\hat{d}^{\mathrm{FIML}}/\hat{d}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}$
with $\hat{d}^{\mathrm{FIML}}$ and $\hat{d}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}$
the disagreement functionals evaluated at $\hat{\theta}$:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{d}^{\mathrm{FIML}} & = & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}\sum_{r\in\mathcal{X}}\hat{\theta}_{r}\,l(r_{j},r_{k}),\\
\hat{d}_{C}^{\mathrm{FIML}} & = & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}\sum_{r,s\in\mathcal{X}}\hat{\theta}_{r}\hat{\theta}_{s}\,l(r_{j},s_{k}).
\end{eqnarray*}
The Fleiss variant sums over all $(j,k)$; the Brennan--Prediger variant
uses $\hat{d}_{BP}$ from \eqref{eq:bp-chance}.
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:fiml-mar}Suppose $\theta$ lies in the interior of the
simplex and the missingness mechanism is MAR. Then $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\kappa_{X}$
for $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$, and
\[
\sqrt{n}\big(\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}-\kappa_{X}\big)\stackrel{d}{\to}N(0,\sigma_{X,\mathrm{FIML}}^{2}),
\]
where $\sigma_{X,\mathrm{FIML}}^{2}$ is the semiparametric efficiency
bound for the smooth functional $\theta\mapsto\kappa_{X}(\theta)$
in the model $\theta\in\Delta^{C^{R}-1}$ with MAR missingness.
\end{prop}

Consistency follows from standard MLE theory and Rubin's ignorability;
efficiency is the discrete-data version of \citet[Theorem 5.39]{Van_der_Vaart2000-qc}.
The asymptotic variance $\sigma_{X,\mathrm{FIML}}^{2}$ is estimated
from Louis' observed-information identity at convergence; see Section
\ref{sec:raw-inference}.

% PARTIALLY MIGRATED to papers/ipw/. The IPW influence-function / V-
% statistic delta-method paragraphs are in Paper A Sec 3.2. The Louis
% observed-information paragraphs (FIML side) are Paper B material.
\subsection{Inference}

\label{sec:raw-inference}

For the IPW estimator, write each functional as a ratio $\hat{\psi}^{N}/\hat{\psi}^{D}$
of $V$-statistic averages and apply the delta method via influence
functions. For the observed disagreement, $\hat{d}^{\mathrm{IPW}}=\hat{\psi}_{dN}/\hat{\psi}_{dD}$
with kernels
\[
h_{dN,i}=\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij},X_{ik})M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1},\qquad h_{dD,i}=\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1},
\]
and the influence functions of the numerator and denominator estimated
by $\hat{\phi}_{dN,i}=h_{dN,i}-\hat{\psi}_{dN}$, $\hat{\phi}_{dD,i}=h_{dD,i}-\hat{\psi}_{dD}$.
The influence function of $\hat{d}^{\mathrm{IPW}}$ is
\[
\hat{\phi}_{d,i}=\hat{\psi}_{dD}^{-1}\!\left(\hat{\phi}_{dN,i}-\hat{d}^{\mathrm{IPW}}\hat{\phi}_{dD,i}\right).
\]
The chance components $\hat{d}_{X}^{\mathrm{IPW}}$ are ratios of
$V$-statistic averages with cross-subject kernels; their influence
functions are obtained from the standard $V$-statistic projection,
\[
\hat{\phi}_{X\bullet,i}=\hat{g}_{X\bullet,1,i}+\hat{g}_{X\bullet,2,i}-2\hat{\psi}_{X\bullet},\qquad\hat{g}_{X\bullet,1,i}=n^{-1}\sum_{i'}K_{X\bullet}(Z_{i},Z_{i'}),
\]
where $Z_{i}=(X_{i\cdot},M_{i\cdot})$ and $\hat{g}_{X\bullet,2,i}$
uses the symmetric kernel evaluation. Combining,
\begin{equation}
\hat{\phi}_{\kappa_{X},i}=-\hat{d}_{X}^{-1}\hat{\phi}_{d,i}+\hat{d}\,\hat{d}_{X}^{-2}\hat{\phi}_{d_{X},i},\label{eq:if-kappa}
\end{equation}
and a consistent estimator of $\sigma_{X,\mathrm{IPW}}^{2}$ is $\hat{\sigma}_{X,\mathrm{IPW}}^{2}=n^{-1}\sum_{i}\hat{\phi}_{\kappa_{X},i}^{2}$.
Joint asymptotic covariances across coefficients follow by replacing
the square with an outer product. For the Brennan--Prediger variant,
$d_{BP}$ is constant and the second term in \eqref{eq:if-kappa}
vanishes.

For the FIML estimator, Louis' observed-information identity gives
\[
\hat{I}_{\mathrm{obs}}=\hat{I}_{\mathrm{full}}-\hat{V}_{\mathrm{miss}},
\]
where $\hat{I}_{\mathrm{full}}$ is the complete-data information
evaluated at $\hat{\theta}$ and $\hat{V}_{\mathrm{miss}}$ is the
posterior variance of the complete-data score given the observed
data. The asymptotic variance of $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}-\kappa_{X})$
is then the delta-method propagation $\nabla\kappa_{X}(\hat{\theta})^{\top}\hat{I}_{\mathrm{obs}}^{-1}\nabla\kappa_{X}(\hat{\theta})$,
using the Moore--Penrose pseudo-inverse when $\theta$ is not fully
identified (see Section \ref{sec:counts}). The same influence-function
framework yields joint asymptotic covariances across coefficients.

\section{Counts data with exchangeable raters}

\label{sec:counts}

Some applications aggregate ratings into category counts and discard
rater identities. For each subject $i$, the data is $N_{i}=(N_{i,1},\dots,N_{i,C})$
with $\sum_{c=1}^{C}N_{i,c}=r_{i}\leq R$, where $r_{i}$ is the number
of raters who rated subject $i$. The model for this section is exchangeable
raters with hypergeometric subsampling.

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (Sec 4.1 -> Paper A "Model and assumptions")
\subsection{Model and assumptions}

\label{sec:counts-model}

Let $Z_{i}=(Z_{i,1},\dots,Z_{i,C})$ with $\sum_{c}Z_{i,c}=R$ denote
the full count vector for subject $i$, obtained from $X_{i}^{\star}$
by aggregating over raters. We assume:
\begin{assump}[Exchangeable raters]
\label{ass:exchange} The joint distribution of $X_{i,1}^{\star},\dots,X_{i,R}^{\star}$
is invariant under permutation of the rater index. Equivalently, the
distribution of $X_{i}^{\star}$ depends only on the composition $Z_{i}$.
\end{assump}
\begin{assump}[Hypergeometric subsampling]
\label{ass:sampling} Given $Z_{i}$ and $r_{i}$, the observed counts
$N_{i}$ are a uniformly-random subsample of size $r_{i}$ from $Z_{i}$,
\[
P(N_{i}\mid Z_{i},r_{i})=\binom{R}{r_{i}}^{-1}\prod_{c=1}^{C}\binom{Z_{i,c}}{N_{i,c}}.
\]
\end{assump}
Under Assumption \ref{ass:exchange}, the full composition is a sufficient
statistic for the joint, and we parametrise the model by $\theta_{z}=P(Z=z)$
on the simplex of compositions of $R$ into $C$ non-negative parts.
The parameter space is strictly larger than the $C$-multinomial
$\{Z\sim\mathrm{Mult}(R,p):p\in\Delta^{C-1}\}$ and includes mixtures
of multinomials.

The Fleiss and Brennan--Prediger disagreement coefficients are linear
functionals of $\theta$:
\begin{equation}
d(\theta)=\sum_{z}\theta_{z}\,d_{z},\qquad d_{z}=\frac{z^{\top}Lz-\mathrm{diag}(L)^{\top}z}{R(R-1)},\label{eq:dz}
\end{equation}
and $d_{F}(\theta)=p(\theta)^{\top}Lp(\theta)$ with $p(\theta)_{c}=\sum_{z}\theta_{z}z_{c}/R$.
The Conger variant requires rater identities and is not in scope for
counts data.

% NOT migrated to Paper A. Remark 1 (identifiability of kappa despite
% non-identified theta) is Paper B material.
\begin{rem}[Identifiability]
\label{rem:identifiability}The parameter $\theta$ is not in general
identified from partial counts: different distributions on full compositions
can produce the same distribution of observed counts $N$. The disagreement
functionals $d(\theta)$ and $p(\theta)$ are linear in $\theta$ and
are identified, so the kappa coefficients are identified even when
$\theta$ is not. Different EM initialisations may converge to different
$\hat{\theta}$ on the same data, but the resulting $\hat{\kappa}_{X}$
is unique.
\end{rem}

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (Sec 4.2 -> Paper A "Pairwise plug-in estimator")
\subsection{Pairwise plug-in estimator}

\label{sec:counts-available}

The pairwise plug-in estimator uses the natural sample analogue of
\eqref{eq:dz}:
\[
\hat{d}^{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\sum_{i}\big(N_{i}^{\top}LN_{i}-\mathrm{diag}(L)^{\top}N_{i}\big)}{\sum_{i}r_{i}(r_{i}-1)},
\]
which divides the observed within-subject pair losses by the number
of observed within-subject pairs. The chance components are
\[
\hat{d}_{F}^{\mathrm{AC}}=\hat{p}^{\top}L\hat{p},\qquad\hat{p}_{c}=\frac{\sum_{i}N_{i,c}}{\sum_{i}r_{i}},
\]
and $\hat{d}_{BP}^{\mathrm{AC}}=d_{BP}$. Under Assumptions \ref{ass:exchange}
and \ref{ass:sampling}, this estimator coincides with the IPW estimator
restricted to counts data (the rater identities have integrated out
under exchangeability).
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:counts-ac}Under Assumptions \ref{ass:exchange} and \ref{ass:sampling},
$\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{AC}}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\kappa_{X}$ for
$X\in\{F,BP\}$ in either of the regimes
\begin{itemize}
\item \emph{MCAR-counts}: $r_{i}\perp Z_{i}$.
\item \emph{MAR-counts}: $P(r_{i}\mid Z_{i})=P(r_{i}\mid N_{i})$.
\end{itemize}
\end{prop}
\begin{proof}[Sketch]
Under hypergeometric subsampling, $E[N_{i,c}\mid r_{i},Z_{i}]=r_{i}Z_{i,c}/R$,
so $\hat{p}_{c}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}p_{c}$ under either regime. A
direct calculation using the second-moment identity of the hypergeometric
gives $E[N_{i}^{\top}LN_{i}-\mathrm{diag}(L)^{\top}N_{i}\mid r_{i},Z_{i}]=r_{i}(r_{i}-1)d_{Z_{i}}/[R(R-1)]\cdot R(R-1)=r_{i}(r_{i}-1)d_{Z_{i}}$,
and the strong law gives convergence of $\hat{d}^{\mathrm{AC}}$ to
$d(\theta)$.
\end{proof}

% NOT migrated to Paper A. Sec 4.3 (counts FIML) is Paper B material.
\subsection{FIML for counts}

\label{sec:counts-fiml}

The observed-data likelihood under Assumptions \ref{ass:exchange}
and \ref{ass:sampling} is
\[
P(N_{i}\mid\theta,r_{i})=\binom{R}{r_{i}}^{-1}\sum_{e}\theta_{N_{i}+e}\prod_{c=1}^{C}\binom{N_{i,c}+e_{c}}{N_{i,c}},
\]
where the sum runs over compositions $e$ of $R-r_{i}$ into $C$
non-negative parts and $\binom{R}{r_{i}}$ is a $\theta$-free constant
dropped from the optimisation. The EM algorithm follows from the standard
multinomial-mixture structure: in the E-step, posterior weights are
\[
q_{i,e}^{(t)}=\frac{\theta_{N_{i}+e}^{(t)}\prod_{c}\binom{N_{i,c}+e_{c}}{N_{i,c}}}{\sum_{e'}\theta_{N_{i}+e'}^{(t)}\prod_{c}\binom{N_{i,c}+e'_{c}}{N_{i,c}}},
\]
and the M-step sets $\theta_{z}^{(t+1)}=n^{-1}\sum_{i}q_{i,z-N_{i}}^{(t)}\mathbf{1}[z\geq N_{i}]$.
The FIML kappa is then the plug-in evaluation of $\kappa_{X}$ at
the EM limit $\hat{\theta}$.
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:counts-fiml}Under Assumptions \ref{ass:exchange}, \ref{ass:sampling},
and MAR-counts, the EM iterate $\hat{\theta}$ converges to a maximiser
of the observed-data likelihood, and the resulting $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\kappa_{X}$
for $X\in\{F,BP\}$. The limit $\hat{\theta}$ may depend on initialisation
when $\theta$ is not identified, but $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}$
does not.
\end{prop}

The MCAR / MAR distinction for counts data is narrower than for rater-identified
data. Both the pairwise plug-in and FIML are consistent in either
regime; in finite samples, FIML may have lower variance when the row
sums $r_{i}$ vary substantially across subjects, because it downweights
short rows whose $N_{i}$ pins down $Z_{i}$ less tightly. When $r_{i}\equiv R$,
the two estimators coincide.

% PARTIALLY MIGRATED to papers/ipw/. Paper A's counts pairwise plug-in
% inference is folded into Sec 4.2 as a one-paragraph pointer to Sec 3.2.
% The FIML Moore-Penrose paragraph here is Paper B material.
\subsection{Inference for counts}

\label{sec:counts-inference}

For the pairwise plug-in estimator, the influence-function framework
of Section \ref{sec:raw-inference} applies with $V$-statistic kernels
defined on the observed counts. For the FIML estimator, Louis' identity
on the multinomial-mixture log-likelihood gives the observed information,
and the delta-method propagation uses the Moore--Penrose pseudo-inverse
on the rank-deficient information matrix when $\theta$ is not identified
(Remark \ref{rem:identifiability}); the pseudo-inverse selects the
minimum-norm solution in the unidentified directions, and the resulting
asymptotic variance of $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{FIML}}$ is invariant
to the choice of representative.

\section{Other estimators}

\label{sec:other}

Two estimators that appear in applied practice, and one closed-form
special case, are recorded here for comparison.

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (Sec 5.1 -> Paper A Sec 5.1, reframed around
% AC inconsistency under non-exchangeable raters per Exp 03).
\subsection{Available-case (raw)}

\label{sec:other-ac}

The available-case (pairwise complete) estimator $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{AC}}=1-\hat{d}^{\mathrm{AC}}/\hat{d}_{X}^{\mathrm{AC}}$
averages observed-pair losses with unit weights,
\[
\hat{d}^{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\sum_{i}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{ik}}{\sum_{i}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}},\qquad\hat{d}_{C}^{\mathrm{AC}}=\frac{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{i'k}}{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{i'k}}.
\]
With the convention $l(\cdot,\mathrm{NA})=l(\mathrm{NA},\cdot)=0$,
the indicator factors absorb into the loss arguments.
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:ac-consistency}The available-case estimator is strongly
consistent in either of the following regimes.
\begin{enumerate}
\item Raters are exchangeable and the missingness is MCAR.
\item Raters are not exchangeable and the missingness probabilities $\pi_{j}$
and $\pi_{jk}$ are constant in $(j,k)$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{prop}

The proof is in Appendix \ref{sec:appendix-ac}. Outside these regimes
the available-case estimator is generally inconsistent. Even within
them it is inefficient: it places equal weight on every observed pair,
while the efficient estimator weights each pair inversely to its observation
probability. The bias-and-efficiency picture is illustrated by simulation
in Section \ref{sec:simulations}.

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (Sec 5.2 -> Paper A Sec 5.2)
\subsection{Gwet's reweighting}

\label{sec:other-gwet}

The default missing-data behaviour of the \texttt{irrCAC} package
\citep{Gwet2014-pj} corresponds to a hybrid estimator $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{\mathrm{G}}$
that uses the available-case observed disagreement and an inverse-marginal
reweighting of the chance disagreement: $\hat{d}^{\mathrm{G}}=\hat{d}^{\mathrm{AC}}$,
and
\[
\hat{d}_{C}^{\mathrm{G}}=\frac{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}}{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}},
\]
with $\hat{d}_{F}^{\mathrm{G}}$ defined analogously and $\hat{d}_{BP}^{\mathrm{G}}=d_{BP}$.
Gwet's estimator coincides with the available-case estimator under
MCAR with constant $\pi_{j}$, and inherits the bias of the available-case
observed-disagreement component when the missingness mechanism varies
across raters.

% NOT migrated to Paper A. Sec 5.3 (closed-form quadratic) is Paper C
% material, where it anchors the Lin's-CCC framing.
\subsection{Closed-form quadratic estimator}

\label{sec:other-quadratic}

For the quadratic loss $l(x,y)=(x-y)^{2}$ on numeric category scores,
the chance-corrected coefficient admits a closed-form moment estimator
based on per-rater means and covariances of the observed entries.
On pairwise-complete data (each subject rated by exactly two raters,
under exchangeability), the moment-based estimator attains the semiparametric
efficiency bound: the condition $E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})\mid X_{j},X_{k}]$
is constant in $(X_{j},X_{k})$ in that setting, which is the necessary
and sufficient condition for moment efficiency \citep[see][]{Tsiatis2006-sx}.
For partially-observed data the estimator remains consistent under
MCAR (per-rater means and covariances are identified from observed
entries) but no longer efficient outside the two-rater special case;
in those settings FIML on the discretised data is the recommended
estimator.

% PARTIALLY MIGRATED to papers/ipw/. Paper A keeps IPW / AC / Gwet
% rows only and drops DGP C (MAR; Paper B). Table regeneration is
% pending the script split.
\section{Simulations}

\label{sec:simulations}

A small Monte Carlo study illustrates the bias and efficiency ordering
predicted by Sections \ref{sec:raw}--\ref{sec:other}. The full
design and tables for additional loss families and coefficient variants
are reported in the online supplement; we summarise the headline cells
here. The data-generating process draws a latent truth $T_{i}\sim\mathrm{Categorical}(p)$
and, for each rater $j$, generates the rating as $T_{i}$ with probability
$\rho_{ij}$ and otherwise a guess from $p$. Three DGPs cover the
intended comparisons:
\begin{itemize}
\item \emph{DGP A.} Exchangeable raters, MCAR missingness with rater-varying
$\pi_{j}$. All four estimators target $\kappa_{X}$.
\item \emph{DGP B.} Non-exchangeable raters, MCAR missingness with rater-varying
$\pi_{j}$. AC and Gwet are biased; IPW and FIML are unbiased.
\item \emph{DGP C.} Difficulty-dependent skill, MAR via the latent truth.
IPW, AC, and Gwet are biased; FIML is approximately unbiased.
\end{itemize}
\input{tables/simulations-raw-three-estimators-main.tex}

The headline takeaways match the theory. FIML is the only estimator
unbiased across all three regimes. IPW is unbiased under MCAR (DGPs
A and B) but biased under MAR (DGP C). AC and Gwet acquire bias whenever
raters are non-exchangeable (DGP B) or missingness is informative
(DGP C). Under exchangeable raters with MCAR (DGP A) all four estimators
are consistent, with comparable Monte Carlo SDs.
Additional tables for the linear loss, Fleiss, and Brennan--Prediger
variants are in \texttt{supplement/simulations-raw-three-estimators-appendix.tex}.

% PARTIALLY MIGRATED to papers/ipw/. Paper A keeps IPW / AC / Gwet
% paths only; the counts FIML example becomes a counts "available"
% example. Paper B will document the FIML paths.
\section{The R package \texttt{misskappa}}

\label{sec:rpackage}

The methods are implemented in the R package \texttt{misskappa}. The
single user-facing entry point is \texttt{kappa()} for raw rating
matrices and \texttt{kappa\_counts()} for counts data; both accept
a \texttt{method} argument with values \texttt{"available"}, \texttt{"ipw"},
\texttt{"fiml"}, or \texttt{"gwet"} (counts data supports \texttt{"available"}
and \texttt{"fiml"}). Generic methods \texttt{coef()}, \texttt{vcov()},
and \texttt{confint()} are provided.

Example (raw ratings with missing values). The dataset \texttt{dat.gwet2014}
included with the package contains $n=20$ items rated by $R=5$ raters
with several missing ratings:
\begin{verbatim}
library(misskappa)
data(dat.gwet2014)
x <- as.matrix(dat.gwet2014)
fit_ipw  <- kappa(x, method = "ipw",  weight = "identity")
fit_fiml <- kappa(x, method = "fiml", weight = "identity")
coef(fit_fiml)
confint(fit_fiml, level = 0.95)
\end{verbatim}
Example (counts data). The dataset \texttt{dat.fleiss1971} provides
Fleiss' original count-form example with $n=30$ subjects, $C=5$ categories,
and $r_{i}\equiv6$:
\begin{verbatim}
data(dat.fleiss1971)
kappa_counts(dat.fleiss1971, method = "fiml",
             weight = "identity", r_total = 6)
\end{verbatim}
With $r_{i}\equiv R$ the pairwise plug-in and FIML coincide; the
example is included to demonstrate the call shape.

% PARTIALLY MIGRATED to papers/ipw/. Paper A's conclusion lands on
% IPW with pointers to companion papers; the FIML / quadratic
% paragraphs migrate to Papers B and C respectively.
\section{Concluding remarks}

\label{sec:remarks}

The four estimators in Sections \ref{sec:raw} and \ref{sec:counts}
cover the cases most commonly encountered in agreement studies with
missing raters. Practical guidance reduces to two checks. First, identify
the data form: rater-identified ratings admit the full IPW / FIML
pair, while counts data restricts the analysis to the exchangeable-rater
model. Second, weigh the missingness assumption: under MCAR the IPW
estimator is consistent and has a closed-form influence-function
variance, while under MAR the FIML estimator is required for consistency
and additionally achieves semiparametric efficiency. The available-case
and Gwet estimators are convenient defaults but consistent only under
narrower assumptions, and inefficient where they are consistent. The
closed-form quadratic estimator is the one setting where pairwise
plug-in estimation attains efficiency.

A natural follow-up is the corresponding study for continuous ratings
under MAR, where the discrete-data FIML construction has no immediate
analogue and semiparametric efficient estimation requires modelling
the observation propensity. The \texttt{misskappa} package implements
continuous-data versions of IPW and the available-case estimator;
the efficiency theory is left for future work.

\bibliographystyle{plainnat}
\bibliography{kappa-missing}

\appendix

% MIGRATED to papers/ipw/ (Appendix proof of Prop 5)
\section{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:ac-consistency}}

\label{sec:appendix-ac}
\begin{proof}[Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:ac-consistency}]
We sketch the argument for $\hat{\kappa}_{C}^{\mathrm{AC}}$; the
Fleiss and Brennan--Prediger versions are analogous. Define
\[
C_{jk}(x^{\star})=P(M_{j}M_{k}=1\mid X^{\star}=x^{\star}),\qquad C_{j}(x^{\star})=P(M_{j}=1\mid X^{\star}=x^{\star}).
\]
Then $E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})M_{j}M_{k}]=E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})C_{jk}(X^{\star})]$
and $E[M_{j}M_{k}]=E[C_{jk}(X^{\star})]$. By the law of large numbers
and the continuous mapping theorem,
\[
\hat{d}^{\mathrm{AC}}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\frac{\sum_{j<k}E\!\left[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})C_{jk}(X^{\star})\right]}{\sum_{j<k}E\!\left[C_{jk}(X^{\star})\right]}.
\]
Let $Y^{\star}$ be an independent copy of $X^{\star}$. A similar
calculation gives
\[
\hat{d}_{C}^{\mathrm{AC}}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\frac{\sum_{j<k}E\!\left[l(X_{j}^{\star},Y_{k}^{\star})C_{j}(X^{\star})C_{k}(Y^{\star})\right]}{\sum_{j<k}E\!\left[C_{j}(X^{\star})\right]E\!\left[C_{k}(Y^{\star})\right]}.
\]
Under MCAR with exchangeable raters, $C_{jk}(X^{\star})=\pi_{jk}$
and $C_{j}(X^{\star})=\pi_{j}$ are constants and $E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})]$
does not depend on $(j,k)$; the constants cancel and the limits reduce
to $d$ and $d_{C}$. Under non-exchangeable raters with constant
$\pi_{j},\pi_{jk}$ the same cancellation holds. The displayed limits
also exhibit the failure mode: when $C_{jk}(X^{\star})$ or $C_{j}(X^{\star})$
covaries with the loss, the limit is a reweighted average and not
the desired parameter.
\end{proof}
\begin{rem}
The same calculation applied to Gwet's estimator (Section \ref{sec:other-gwet})
replaces $C_{j}(X^{\star})C_{k}(Y^{\star})$ in the chance denominator
by its product with $\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}$. Under
MCAR the reweighting cancels; under more general mechanisms the limit
is a reweighted target and does not in general equal $d_{X}$.
\end{rem}

\end{document}
